text: The precise bearing of this proverb remains in doubt.
The whole of this verse is obscure, and the second half of it probably corrupt.
The Hebrew text reads simply, ‘As water face to face, so heart of man to man’, it is not clear in what sense.

That common repute should be regarded as a test of what a man really is, seems unlikely (cp. especially 17.3 above). Some think the Hebrew text means a man should be judged by what he holds in repute, and this would fit in well with the second part of the verse (which is found in the Septuagint Greek, but not in the Hebrew text itself). Others would read, instead of ‘repute’, ‘adversity’, and this would lead on well to verse 22.
The Latin version here ignores the question, and runs ‘but a crown shall be granted for ever’; a rendering which yields no good sense in the context.
